entries.title: BreachForums logs reveal anonymizers of choice for shady characters
entries.summary: <p>When threat actors log into systems, they don't use their real IP addresses. Instead, they route their traffic through VPNs, proxies, and&nbsp;anonymizing networks. We analyzed a January 2026 data leak from BreachForums - a major English-language cybercrime marketplace - that contained threat actor IP addresses. The analysis can help Okta administrators make evidenced-based decisions about risky login behaviors and how to use controls to limit logins from dodgy virtual private network (VPN) services or anonymizing networks.</p> <p>IP addresses are key indicators of compromise. Attackers don't want to use IP addresses that could be potentially linked to their real-world identities if law enforcement obtains warrants or subpoenas that will be honored by service providers. This pushes them to the corners: virtual private network (VPN) services that promise to not retain logs, anonymizing proxies and so-called "bulletproof" networks. The desire to protect their identity extends not only to when they are staging attacks but also when threat actors log into forums and marketplaces used for cybercriminal activity.</p> <p>Underground forums occasionally experience data breaches, and these leaks are an informative source of open-source intelligence. In January 2026, a dataset from a cybercriminal forum known as BreachForums was publicly released. The data compromised registered users from the forum, including nicknames, hashed passwords, email addresses, and IP addresses, with the data current as of around October 2025.&nbsp;</p> <p>BreachForums was a mainstay of the English-speaking cybercriminal underground, offering tools and services for fraud and cybercrime, guides, malware and served as an advertising platform for selling data leaks. It was the successor of RaidForums, which was <a href="https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/united-states-leads-seizure-one-world-s-largest-hacker-forums-and-ar

entries.title: LiteLLM supply chain attack: an explainer for identity pros
entries.summary: <p>In June 2025, Okta Threat Intelligence predicted that the rapid adoption of AI agents would generate <a href="https://www.okta.com/blog/threat-intelligence/the-secrets-agentic-ai-leaves-behind/">"identity debt"</a> as developers experimented with these new technologies.</p> <p>This scenario has played out in numerous software supply chain attacks over the last six months, in which attacker payloads search compromised developer systems for plaintext secrets in configuration files and exfiltrate them to attacker-controlled servers.</p> <p>The latest of these attacks - and one of the most consequential - targeted <a href="https://docs.litellm.ai/blog/security-update-march-2026">users of LiteLLM</a>.</p> <h2>What is LiteLLM, and what went wrong?</h2> <p>LiteLLM is most often used by developers as a gateway for client applications to call any number of large language models (2000+) sourced from over 100 providers.</p> <p>This offers software developers an ability to perform A/B testing of different models, build redundancy into agentic apps (swap to a secondary model if a primary is unavailable or is rate limited), as well as use cases concerned with auditing and cost control.</p> <p>On March 24, 2026, threat actors known as TeamPCP pushed <a href="https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/issues/24512">malicious updates to the LiteLLM PyPI package</a> (versions 1.82.7 and 1.82.8).&nbsp;</p> <p>These updates install a file on the compromised device (see indicators below), which executes a malicious script every time Python starts on the device. The script essentially functions as an infostealer - it seeks out and stages environment variables (API tokens, secrets, tokens), SSH keys, Git keys and CI/CD secrets, cloud credentials (AWS, GCP, Azure keys), database credentials, kubernetes secrets and SSL/TLS keys. It also

entries.title: Disrupting ShieldGuard: a security extension primed to drain crypto wallets
entries.summary: <h2>Executive summary</h2> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has discovered and helped industry partners to take down the infrastructure of a cryptocurrency scam called "ShieldGuard".</p> <p>ShieldGuard claims to be a blockchain project that offers - through its promotion of a browser extension - a capability that blocks known threats to cryptocurrency wallets, such as phishing or malicious smart contracts.&nbsp;</p> <p>The project was promoted using a multi-level marketing campaign in which users would be rewarded for early use of the extension (via a cryptocurrency "airdrop") and for promoting the capability to other users.</p> <p>Our analysis of the browser extension, presented in detail below, revealed its true intent: ShieldGuard appears designed to harvest wallet addresses and other sensitive data for major cryptocurrency platforms including Binance, Coinbase, MetaMask, OpenSea, Phantom and Uniswap, as well as for users of Google services.&nbsp;</p> <p>The extension also extracts the full HTML of pages after a user signs into Binance, Coinbase, OpenSea or Uniswap via their browser.</p> <h2>Threat analysis</h2> <p>ShieldGuard was promoted via a public website as a legitimate security application for users of Web3 services.&nbsp;</p> <p>The creators of ShieldGuard also registered:</p> <ul> <li><p>A listing in the Google Chrome Store</p> </li> <li><p>A social media profile at x[.]com</p> </li> <li><p>A Telegram channel</p> </li> </ul> <p>The web site and associated social media profiles for ShieldGuard claimed the extension would detect suspicious transactions prior to a user signing a request.</p> <p>The creators of the browser extension attempted to drive downloads by launching an "airdrop".&nbsp;</p> <p>An ai

entries.title: Vietnam-based cybercrime markets enable account sign-ups at scale
entries.summary: <h2>Executive summary</h2> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence, working with our partners at the University of Cyprus, have connected a cluster of fraudulent account registration activity to a sprawling cybercrime ecosystem based in Vietnam.</p> <p>Fraudulent online accounts are more than just a nuisance; they are a critical tool for large-scale financial fraud. From spam to phishing to devastating interpersonal fraud scams, these accounts provide a veneer of legitimacy that allows criminals to abuse platforms and customers of those platforms.&nbsp;</p> <p>In late 2025, Okta Threat Intelligence&nbsp;<a href="/content/okta-www/us/en-us/blog/threat-intelligence/opportunistic-sms-pumping-attacks-target-customer-sign-up-pages.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">investigated</a> signup fraud campaigns using infrastructure clusters internally designated as O-UNC-036 that relied on disposable email addresses in order to execute SMS pumping attacks, which is also known as International Revenue Sharing Fraud (IRSF).&nbsp; In this scheme, malicious actors automate the creation of puppet accounts in a targeted service provider. Fraudsters use these account registrations to trigger SMS messages to premium rate phone numbers and profit from charges incurred. This activity can prove costly for service providers who use SMS to verify registration information in customer accounts or to send multifactor authentication (MFA) security codes.</p> <p>In the course of this investigation, Okta Threat Intelligence and our partners observed links from O-UNC-036 to dozens of websites that cater to individuals who want to conduct online fraud. This cybercrime-as-a-service (CaaS) ecosystem provides paid infrastructure and services that make it easier for other individuals to conduct online fraud.&nbsp; Many of these online storefronts sell user accounts that have been hijacked or creat

entries.title: Universities exposed to account takeover risk from contract cheating services
entries.summary: <h2>Executive Summary</h2> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has identified extortion campaigns that target university students.</p> <p>These operations often masquerade as &quot;tutoring" or "proctoring" services, but function as <a href="https://www.teqsa.gov.au/preventing-contract-cheating/what-contract-cheating-and-methods-reduce-it" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">contract cheating operations</a> that feed sophisticated identity-theft and financial-crime rackets.&nbsp;</p> <p>These <a href="https://www.insidehighered.com/news/students/academics/2024/03/28/sting-operation-fools-proctoring-service-blackmail-attempted" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">extortion campaigns</a> appear to first involve local and online recruiting efforts that seek students as clients.</p> <p>In order for third parties to complete academic work on behalf of a student, students are asked to facilitate access for the proctoring services to academic systems, in some cases by sharing authentication credentials.&nbsp;</p> <p>These third parties then press the students for further payment by threatening to expose the student for cheating.</p> <p>The extortionists have been observed logging in from VPNs, residential, and mobile IP addresses in Kenya.</p> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has collaborated with several universities and people to study this activity, including Glen Woolley, Andrew Tolhurst, and Damien Mathieson of the Cyber Security Operations team at the University of Sydney.</p> <p>This is not merely a matter of academic integrity; it is a <a href="https://www.qaa.ac.uk/docs/qaa/guidance/contracting-to-cheat-in-higher-education-third-edition.pdf">threat to student safety and standards</a>, the university's security perimeter and as one research institu

entries.title: The North Korean on your payroll
entries.summary: <p>In September 2025, Okta Threat Intelligence <a href="https://www.okta.com/newsroom/articles/north-korea-s-it-workers-expand-beyond-us-big-tech/">published research</a> from a large-scale analysis into fraudulent employment schemes conducted by Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK) IT Workers (ITW).&nbsp;</p> <p>That research collated data from over 130 actors, conducting over 6500 interviews with 500 companies.&nbsp;</p> <p>In this post, we look specifically at the activities of two individual personas. We selected these two examples from a large list of actors that we continue to track because they exemplify the typical tools, techniques and procedures (TTPs) employed by DPRK ITW actors. Additionally, each had novel observables that can further inform defenders against these efforts.</p> <p>These two actors reveal two interesting TTPs DPRK actors use to land employment: the abuse of legitimate LinkedIn profiles to pass reference checks, and the abuse of stolen identities.</p> <h2>#1 - Meet "JJ"&nbsp;</h2> <p>The first of the two actors we will detail we'll refer to as "JJ". This actor has prolifically interviewed for roles in multiple verticals over two years, with an overrepresentation of roles in AI and healthcare.<br> </p> <p>The email account used by this actor is similar in structure to other DPRK-linked actors, in that it utilizes a free webmail service and the account name incorporates references to software development and other randomized alphanumeric characters. Open-source intelligence (OSINT) research conducted into the email address used by this actor uncovered a number of online services accounts that are very typical of DPRK ITW actors. All of these accounts are used exclusively for job applications and associated tasks:</p> <ul> <li><p>Job board and hiring platforms</p> &l

entries.title: Detecting OpenClaw using advanced posture checks
entries.summary: <p>OpenClaw is a free and open-source "personal AI assistant" that a user can connect to the local resources on their computer, messaging applications, calendars, and <a href="https://www.okta.com/newsroom/articles/agents-run-amok--identity-lessons-from-moltbook-s-ai-experiment/">anything else they want the agent to access</a>.</p> <p>Right now, at least one of your users is experimenting with a personal assistant like OpenClaw. How you feel about that depends largely on the risk appetite of your organization, and how quickly you can spin up resources to assess the risk of this week's AI buzztoy. This stuff is coming at us fast.</p> <p>A "personal AI assistant" doesn't need to be malicious or vulnerable for you to want to wrap some policy around its use on corporate-issued devices.&nbsp;</p> <p>The facts are:<br> </p> <ul> <li><p>Personal AI assistants, by their nature, will seek broad system access - interacting with files, processes, and network resources - which makes it a powerful tool if it were ever abused.&nbsp;</p> </li> <li><p>Personal AI assistants may install persistence mechanisms like launchd services and binaries across multiple paths, making them difficult to fully remove.&nbsp;</p> </li> <li><p>The default listening port for these personal assistants could be exploited for remote access to your device or command-and-control.&nbsp;</p> </li> </ul> <p>The most conservative option would be to block the use of these technologies until your team has had some time to figure out how to use them safely. (See these <a href="https://auth0.com/blog/five-step-guide-securing-moltbot-ai-agent/">tips from the Auth0 team</a> if you're experimenting with OpenClaw).&nbsp;</p> <p>You may also want to make access decisions for spe

entries.title: Phishing kits adapt to the script of callers
entries.summary: <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has detected and dissected multiple custom phishing kits that have evolved to meet the specific needs of voice-based social engineers ("callers") in vishing campaigns.</p> <p>These custom kits are made available on an as-a-service basis and are increasingly used by a growing number of intrusion actors targeting Google, Microsoft, Okta and a range of cryptocurrency providers.</p> <p>The kits are capable of intercepting the credentials of targeted users, while also presenting the supporting context required to convince users to approve MFA challenges, or to take other actions in the interests of the attacker on the phone. They can be adapted on the fly by callers to control what pages are presented in the user's browser, in order to sync with the caller's script and whatever legitimate MFA challenges the caller is presented with as they attempt to sign-in.</p> <p>"Once you get into the driver's seat of one of these tools, you can immediately see why we are observing higher volumes of voice-based social engineering," said Moussa Diallo, threat researcher at Okta Threat Intelligence. "Using these kits, an attacker on the phone to a targeted user can control the authentication flow as that user interacts with credential phishing pages. They can control what pages the target sees in their browser in perfect synchronization with the instructions they are providing on the call. The threat actor can use this synchronization to defeat any form of MFA that is not phishing-resistant."</p> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has published a <a href="https://security.okta.com/product/okta/vishing-operators-synchronize-phishing-sites-to-their-script-for-hybrid-social-engineering-attacks">detailed threat advisory</a> for customers that provides an inside look at the capabilities of two such kits used by intrusion actors. This blog post summarizes the key

entries.title: Jobseekers exploited in fake recruiter phishing campaigns
entries.summary: <h2>Executive Summary&nbsp;</h2> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has identified multiple clusters of high-volume&nbsp;phishing activity impersonating recruitment teams from various companies,&nbsp;using over 400 domains to facilitate credential theft.</p> <p>We track this activity as O-UNC-038.</p> <p>In this report we detail:</p> <ul> <li>A campaign using a "Browser in the Browser" (BitB) technique to mimic a Facebook social login, using Telegram bots for credential exfiltration.</li> <li>A campaign specifically targeting Google Workspace corporate account credentials, using Socket.IO for credential exfiltration</li> <li>Similar recruitment-themed phishing campaigns</li> </ul> <p>The primary objective across all of these campaigns is credential harvesting.</p> <p>While more sophisticated campaigns may leverage Adversary-in-the-Middle&nbsp;(AitM) techniques which capture session cookies to bypass Multi-Factor&nbsp;Authentication (MFA), the campaigns in this advisory conduct simple&nbsp;credential harvesting via static phishing kits.</p> <h2>Threat Analysis</h2> <h3>Multiple campaigns, similar targeting</h3> <p>We uncovered and tracked multiple campaigns that all use similar&nbsp;recruitment-themed phishing lures and abuse the same services for lure&nbsp;delivery or tracking.</p> <p>In phishing pages associated with the clusters of activity described below,&nbsp;we observed tracking links associated with Salesforce ExactTarget:</p> <pre> cl.s12[.]exct[.]net/?qs=&lt;UniqueIdentifierString&gt; </pre> <p>Phishing actors commonly abuse email marketing platforms to send phishing&nbsp;emails, either by setting up trial accounts for fake organizations, or by taking&nbsp;over the accounts of email marketing platform customers. The phishi

entries.title: Phishing campaigns use 'Employee Benefits' lure to intercept Microsoft and Okta logins
entries.summary: <h2>Executive Summary</h2> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has identified multi-stage phishing campaigns targeting organizations that use Microsoft applications and - where access to these apps are federated -&nbsp; Okta as Identity Provider (IdP).</p> <p>Our findings, first researching a distinct campaign which we label O-UNC-037, subsequently led us to several other campaigns using the same phishing kit and a very similar lure theme. However, diversity in the infrastructure and style suggests that while these use the same kit and theme, they are likely separate campaigns run by other threat actors. This report focuses on O-UNC-037, although we share details of the other campaigns in the appendix at the end of this report.</p> <p>The O-UNC-037 campaign has been active since at least October 2025, and largely targets technology and industrial supply organizations. It delivers phishing emails using an <b>employee benefits or human resources (HR)</b> themed lure to deceive users into entering their credentials on a fake Microsoft sign-in page.</p> <p>The phishing infrastructure uses Adversary-in-the-Middle (AitM) techniques to intercept authentication flows in real-time, capturing credentials, MFA codes and any session tokens established during the sign-in event. This capability can bypass the protection of several common MFA methods, such as SMS codes and one-time passwords (OTP) from authenticator apps.&nbsp;</p> <p>For organizations that use Okta for Single Sign-On (SSO), the attack then escalates to a second stage, redirecting victims to a malicious replay of an Okta sign-in page on the second-stage phishing domain, which acts as a relay server to additionally capture their Okta credentials and steal the session cookie.</p> <p>&nbsp;</p> <h2>Threat Analysis</h2> <p>This phishing campaign uses a multi-stage attack technique. It specifically targ

entries.title: Wide-scale, opportunistic SMS pumping attacks target customer sign-up pages
entries.summary: <h2>Executive Summary</h2> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has identified a cluster of shared disposable email infrastructure and commodity proxy services, internally designated as O-UNC-036, that is being used to launch high-volume, automated attempts against public API endpoints.</p> <p>This infrastructure has been observed in multiple persistent, large scale and&nbsp;financially motivated SMS pumping campaigns starting at least as&nbsp;early as July 2025.</p> <p>To execute this attack, threat actors undertake the following sequence of&nbsp;actions:</p> <ol> <li>Create a new account using a disposable email address, often tied to a&nbsp;set of domains<br> </li> <li>Add an actor-controlled phone number as an authentication factor<br> </li> <li>Send as many messages to the number as possible in order to achieve&nbsp;their monetary objectives</li> </ol> <p>These attacks generate significant financial costs for the target&nbsp;organizations by running up bills with their telephony providers.&nbsp;We have been able to track historical activity from this cluster of disposable&nbsp;email domains back to at least March 2024, indicating a sustained, adaptive&nbsp;effort. Due to the high financial risk and potential for service degradation, we&nbsp;strongly recommend the immediate implementation of the protective&nbsp;controls, monitoring and aggressive response outlined in this report.</p> <h2>Threat&nbsp;Analysis</h2> <p>The primary objective of this campaign is opportunistic, large-scale account&nbsp;creation in order to carry out SMS pumping campaigns. In these attacks,&nbsp;threat actors profit by collaborating with high-cost international or premium-rate SMS providers. By exploiting the SMS delivery system of the target&nbsp;identity platform, the attacker triggers messages to

entries.title: The s1ngularity attack: When attackers prompt your AI agents to do their bidding
entries.summary: <p>In mid-September, a software supply chain incident - commonly dubbed the "s1ngularity attack" - marked a turning point in adversary use of Artificial Intelligence.&nbsp;</p> <p>While the initial access vector was familiar - a phishing attack targeting npm package maintainers - the resulting malware was novel. This is one of the first attacks in which local AI agents were weaponized to aid in credential theft.</p> <p>This technique represents a foreboding escalation in attacker ingenuity, moving beyond simple custom scripts to leverage the problem-solving capabilities of AI tools already available on a target's machine.</p> <p>I sat down with Paul McCarty, Head of Research at Safety Cybersecurity - who recently published a detailed analysis of the attack - to discuss his findings, including the AI prompts used to search for secrets and bypass guardrails, and how this threat could evolve in the future. Watch the video above, or continue reading for an overview of what we talked about.</p> <h2>The anatomy of a post-install attack</h2> <p>During the attack, malicious updates were pushed to popular npm packages including <a href="https://www.npmjs.com/package/nx" target="_blank">nx</a>, ultimately resulting in the theft of thousands of developer credentials. A key component of the malware was a post-install script the attacker named telemetry.js.</p> <p>The main function of this script was to execute immediately upon installation and search the compromised host for sensitive files and secrets. Crucially, the attacker introduced a new discovery method, by checking for the presence of local CLI agents for popular Large Language Models (LLMs) - specifically Claude, Gemini, and AWS Q.</p> <p>McCarty describes this as a clear evolution of the &quot;living off the land&quot; approach, where attackers abuse pre-installed, trusted tools

entries.title: EvilProxy phishing campaign leverages convincing impersonations of enterprise applications
entries.summary: <h2>Executive Summary</h2> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has identified a high-volume attack campaign that subsequent research shows has been active since at least March 2025, leveraging the EvilProxy phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) toolkit to execute real-time phishing attacks designed to steal user credentials and active session cookies. We track this campaign as O-UNC-035.</p> <p>This operation targets organizations across numerous sectors, including Finance, Government, Healthcare, and Technology, primarily using highly convincing lures impersonating popular business tools such as SAP Concur and document signing services such as Adobe or DocuSign.</p> <h2>Threat Analysis</h2> <h3>EvilProxy Phishing-as-a-Service</h3> <p>EvilProxy (O-TA-041) is a PhaaS toolkit that uses a reverse-proxy/adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) architecture to intercept victims' logins and session cookies in real time, allowing attackers to bypass multi-factor authentication (MFA) and hijack accounts.</p> <p>Originally observed in 2022 and rapidly productized on dark-web marketplaces, EvilProxy has been widely used in targeted account takeover and Business Email Compromise campaigns because it proxies legitimate sites. This means that users see real MFA prompts, and that the attacker is able to capture tokens and cookies for the attacker to replay. Because EvilProxy is sold and maintained as a service, it scales attacks and lowers the skill barrier for criminals: operators can launch large volumes of tailored phishing campaigns.</p> <h3>Lure mechanisms</h3> <p>We have observed trusted enterprise software and services impersonated to trick users into clicking links and entering credentials:</p> <ul> <li><b>Document signing services (such as Docusign, Adobe Acrobat, Adobe Sign, EchoSign): </b>A consistent theme observed in this campaign was the impersonation of doc

entries.title: North Korea's IT Workers expand beyond US big tech
entries.summary: <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has conducted a large-scale analysis revealing that the Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK) IT worker scheme threatens nearly every industry that hires remote talent.</p> <p>While public reporting has primarily focused on DPRK nationals targeting software development roles at major US technology companies, our analysis shows that this threat is not limited to the tech sector, nor the US. North Korean IT Workers (ITW) now pose a real threat to a wide range of industries. Impacted industries include finance, healthcare, public administration, and professional services across a growing number of countries. This widespread scheme aims to gain illicit employment and - in some cases - steal sensitive data.</p> <p>Just how expansive is this threat? Our analysis of thousands of examples from our sample of DPRK ITW activity found that <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Standard_Industrial_Classification" target="_blank">Information and Technology</a> organizations represent only half of the targeted entities. We also found that over a quarter (27%) of targeted entities are based in countries other than the United States.</p> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence observed examples of DPRK-linked actors progressing through multiple interviews for the same roles. While we are not privy to every organization's hiring and onboarding processes, evidence of post-onboarding corporate activities was observed in multiple organizations across different verticals, supporting the theory that a broad, "scatter-gun" approach to job application and interviewing has been successful enough to make it a worthwhile endeavour for the DPRK regime to continue and expand.&nbsp;</p> <p>It's essential that organizations in all industry sectors and countries are made aware that DPRK-linked actors have applied or are likely to apply for advertised remote

entries.title: Help desks targeted in social engineering campaign targeting HR applications
entries.summary: <h2>Executive Summary</h2> <p>Okta Threat Intelligence has actively tracked a financially-motivated cluster of activity known as <b>O-UNC-034</b> since August 2025 that leverages social engineering of help desk staff to takeover accounts and manipulate data in payroll systems.&nbsp;</p> <p>O-UNC-034 has been observed targeting employees of organizations operating in different industries, including but not limited to Education, Manufacturing and Industrials, Retail and Consumer Services, and Pharmaceuticals and Healthcare. It is one of several clusters of activity tracked by Okta Threat Intelligence and other threat researchers that target HR and payroll applications. Variations on this theme include the use of malvertising (O-TA-54) and AitM phishing (STORM-2657).&nbsp;</p> <p>The primary objective of O-UNC-034 is to manipulate the banking details associated with targeted users in HR systems and payroll-related services.</p> <p>This advisory details the observed Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs) and provides relevant Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) associated with this active threat.&nbsp;</p> <p>This information is provided for informational and intelligence purposes to enable organizations to understand and mitigate the risks posed by this campaign.</p> <h2>Threat Analysis</h2> <p>The threat actor is leveraging social engineering techniques by impersonating legitimate employees.</p> <p>For <b>Initial Access</b>, the threat actor has been observed initiating contact with the target company's IT help desk, masquerading as an employee. They use this impersonation to request password resets for the employee's account.</p> <p>Following a successful password reset event, the threat actor establishes <b>persistence</b> by enrolling their own MFA authenticator to the compromised account. The thre
